schools.summary: 'Knit' is better 'bound up' (as in Genesis 44:30); what drew Jonathan was David's sublime faith, not merely his valour.
Matthew Henry reads a providence: the heir befriends his rival so David's way to the throne is cleared, and love governed by grace overrules all secular regards.
Gill: more passed between them than is recorded; David's prudence and piety knit Jonathan's soul to his at once.
Wesley: Jonathan loved him for his virtues, his service to God, and the likeness of their age and qualities.
Saul took David that day and would let him go home no more — the permanent removal from the sheepfold to the court.
Gill: on the very day he slew the Philistine, Saul took David into his household, not merely his service.
Wesley: the verse shows David had not before had a constant residence at court.
The covenant is rooted in a shared faith in the Invisible King (cf. 1 Samuel 14:6); it endured to the last parting at Ziph (1 Samuel 23:16).
Gill: a solemn bond of mutual support in life and death, which providentially gave David a friend at court to preserve his life.
Matthew Henry: true love desires to be constant; those who love Christ join themselves to him in an everlasting covenant.
The meil (cloak) and maddim (armour-coat, cf. 1 Samuel 17:39) are handed over entire; the exchange of arms was an ancient custom (Homer's Glaucus and Diomedes).
Gill: the gift clothed David as a prince rather than a shepherd for his appearance at court.
Clarke: gifts of robes and arms in token of friendship were frequent in the East and among the ancients.
Matthew Henry: David seen in Jonathan's clothes shows he is 'a Jonathan's second self.'

debates.question: Why does Jonathan give David his robe and arms?

sides.position: a customary rite of friendship, only incidentally clothing him
to enable poorly-clad David to appear at court as a prince

schools.summary: 'Behaved wisely' means acted prudently and prosperously (Joshua 1:8); David pleased even the naturally envious courtiers, disarming all jealousy.
Wesley: 'went out' denotes military expeditions, for which David was abundantly qualified.
Gill: David succeeded in whatsoever business Saul set him.
The return follows the whole campaign, not just Goliath; the women perform the timbrel-dance in strophe and antistrophe (cf. Exodus 15:20; Judges 11:34).
Matthew Henry: David's troubles rise from his triumphs; the comparing song was 'more just than wise.'
Clarke: the shalishim were three-stringed instruments, taken as brass sistrums shaken in the dance.
The couplet is a folk-song refrain (quoted again in 1 Samuel 29:5); Saul hears in it his own rejection — 'only the kingdom more for him.'
Clarke: not literal numbers but a comparison of the odds each faced; Saul fought thousands, David tens of thousands.
Matthew Henry: the song was poetic liberty, not an invidious comparison; it was beneath a prince to resent it.

debates.question: Did the song intend to belittle Saul?

sides.position: no — innocent poetic praise, not an invidious comparison
Saul heard it as a measured slight and a threat to his crown

schools.summary: Ellicott: Saul's 'what can he have more but the kingdom?' is an unwitting prophecy — the rejected king naming the throne he has forfeited.
Matthew Henry: Saul should have owned the praise as just; it was beneath a prince to suspect treason in a song.
Gill: he was galled both by the diminished title 'plain Saul' and chiefly by David's larger share of the slain.

themes: 'what can he have more but the kingdom?'

schools.summary: The verb is a denominative 'to look askance'; this is the hinge of Saul's tragedy, where love and hate begin to alternate.
Gill: Saul watched David's every motion with an envious eye and, per the Targum, 'laid wait for him.'
'Prophesied' here means 'raved'; the Hithpael is never used of true prophecy (the Niphal is), so it was a 'bastard imitation' — a new, murderous phase of Saul's madness.
Wesley: his fits of frenzy returned, the fruit of envy; Clarke: he 'feigned' prayer to mask his murderous intent, then hurled the spear.

sides.position: raving under the evil spirit — a counterfeit of true prophecy
feigned praying, to conceal his murderous design (the reflexive Hithpael)

schools.summary: Ellicott: the verb is better 'brandished/aimed' (Alexandrian LXX and Chaldee 'lifted'), since David would not have risked the chamber again had the spear truly been thrown.
Wesley: a literal cast under the evil spirit's power, repeated later in 1 Samuel 19:10.
Gill: Saul cast the javelin at David out of his hand.

debates.question: Did Saul actually throw the javelin, or only brandish it?

sides.position: he hurled it, meaning to pin David to the wall
he 'brandished/aimed' it — David would not else have returned to the chamber

schools.summary: A stroke of deep psychological truth: Saul's fear springs from the consciousness that the Lord had departed from him and was now with David.
Matthew Henry: as Herod feared John (Mark 6:20), Saul stood in awe of David; the wiser David behaved, the more Saul feared him.
Gill: the Lord protected and prospered David (Targum: 'the Word of the Lord was for his help'), and Saul feared for his life and crown.
Keil and Delitzsch: David's success compelled the promotion, but it only increased his standing with the people and Saul's dread.
Clarke and Wesley: the 'honour' was a pretext to be rid of David and to expose him to the greatest hazards.
Gill: Saul removed him to be out of sight and, as he supposed, out of danger.

debates.question: Was the captaincy a promotion or a disguised removal?

sides.position: a disguised exile meant to rid Saul of David and endanger him
a promotion forced by David's success, which only enlarged him

schools.summary: Gill: 'behaved wisely' also means 'prospered'; the following clause gives the reason — the Lord was with him — and David is here a type of Christ.
Keil and Delitzsch: the blessing of the Lord rested on all David did, so his prudence and success are uniform 'in all his ways.'
David's wisdom leaves Saul no open advantage, so his fear turns to the craftiness of the marriage-snare that follows.
Clarke: Saul feared him because by prudent conduct David daily gained increasing influence.
Gill: Saul could get no advantage against him, for David prospered and grew ever more in the people's favour.
Ellicott: the naming of 'Israel and Judah' hints at a post-Rehoboam compiler, yet the distinction predates the disruption; David, though of Judah, was equally loved in the north.
Gill: the singular verb shows every one of the people loved David, so wholly had he won their hearts.

themes: the future uniter of the tribes

schools.summary: Saul cloaks a plot in the offer of Merab, hoping the Philistines will kill David; 'the Lord's battles' is a noble formula (cf. Numbers 21:14) here abused.
Gill: Saul spoke not in good will but designing to lay a snare.
Clarke (with Calmet): wars are the Lord's only by his command; mixed with worldly ambition they become wars of lust and profanity.
The crux is 'what is my life' (chayyay): Keil takes it as 'condition in life,' Ewald/Lange/Ellicott as 'kindred/family' — the preferable sense.
Wesley: David shows humility and wisdom together, so Saul might see he was far from aspiring to the kingdom.
Gill: surprised but not refusing, David answers with great modesty and humility.

debates.question: What does 'what is my life' (chayyay) mean?

sides.position: my condition or station in life
my family or kindred ('what are my relations?')

schools.summary: Saul's capricious wavering breaks the promise; Merab goes to the wealthy Adriel, and her five sons later fall to Gibeonite blood-revenge (2 Samuel 21:9).
Wesley: an act of great injustice; the marriage was accursed and its children cut off by God's appointment.
Gill: the breach came at the very time Merab should have been given to David.
Michal's love gives Saul a fresh excuse to imperil David while seeming to keep his promise; David, wary of Saul's fickleness, makes no reply.
Gill: Michal loved David for his person, gallant behaviour, wise conduct, and reputation.
Saul offers Michal directly and through his servants as 'a second way' to be son-in-law; Ellicott wonders if the plot later suggested to David the murder of Uriah (2 Samuel 11:15).
Gill: the snare was overturned — Michal became the means of David's deliverance (1 Samuel 19:12).
Clarke: the condition was a hundred Philistines slain, in which Saul supposed David must perish.
The private message reconciles Saul's offering the daughter himself with his employing courtiers; Lange notes the servants' 'flattering, conciliatory tone.'
Gill: David, ill-used over Merab, would not listen, so Saul took the method of persuasion by his servants.
David sincerely pleads his poverty — unable to offer a fitting dowry — and reads his loss of Merab as owing to his poverty against Adriel's wealth.
Gill: the servants spoke Saul's words plainly in David's ears for him to weigh.

themes: virtue as the lever of the trap

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the report prompts Saul to name a price of blood — a hundred Philistine foreskins (no ordinary dowry, cf. Genesis 34:12) — to make David fall by the Philistines.
Gill: the servants reported David's sense of unworthiness and inability to bring a suitable dowry.
The foreskins (not heads) betray Saul's suspicion that David might kill Israelites, judging David by himself; Josephus exaggerates the price to 600 heads.
Clarke: the foreskins prove both the number slain and that they were uncircumcised, a custom paralleled among the Abyssinians (Bruce).
Gill: the demand was framed as Saul's answer to remove David's objection about a dowry.
Ellicott: Saul's design spurred David to wilder feats, yet the ferocious cruelty of the offer betrays the half-barbarous age.
Wesley: 'the days' were the set term Saul allowed David for the exploit.
Gill: the servants reported the terms, and the offer pleased David well.
Keil and Delitzsch: David slew two hundred (the Hebrew), delivered the foreskins in full, and won Michal, so Saul saw the Lord was with him.
Gill: the doubled number showed obedience, zeal, and sincere affection; the Greek version has only a hundred.
Wesley: David doubled the number to bind Saul to his promise; Clarke argues a hundred is the true reading (LXX; 2 Samuel 3:14).

debates.question: Did David slay two hundred Philistines or one hundred?

sides.position: two hundred — a generous overplus (the Hebrew text)
one hundred — the true reading (LXX and 2 Samuel 3:14)

schools.summary: The failure of the murderous plan forces on Saul the conviction that Jehovah was with David and protecting him, so his fear grows into lifelong enmity.
Gill: Saul saw God's hand in David's favour and success, and in the overruling of every plot for David's good (Targum: 'the Word of the Lord for his help').
Keil and Delitzsch: David's continual success against the Philistines turns Saul's inward dread into open, lifelong enmity.
Gill: Saul feared the more because the Lord was with David and Michal loved him, sensing the marriage would pave David's way to the throne.

David acts more wisely than all Saul's servants in the renewed Philistine war (probably to avenge v.27), and his name is held in great honour; the raid may have counted on the marriage-exemption (Deuteronomy 24:5).
Gill: David understood the exemption law better than the Philistines, knowing it applied not to a commanded war; so their hope failed.
Clarke and Wesley: the Philistines went forth provoked by their losses and by David's exploit; David proved more skillful than all Saul's officers.
